data.title: Not with us, not against us: The rise of strategic non-alignment
data.description:
In Rio de Janeiro‚Äôs Itamaraty Palace, Brazilian President¬ÝLuiz In√°cio Lula da Silva¬Ýraised a quiet toast. There was no anti-American rhetoric, no thunderous declarations ‚Äî just a call for ‚Äúcooperation without coercion.‚Äù As BRICS leaders gathered for their July 6‚Äì7, 2025, summit, the message was unmistakable: this was not about replacing the United States, but about creating space from it.

In President Trump‚Äôs second term, global diplomacy is not erupting into confrontation. It‚Äôs slipping into something quieter, more deliberate and perhaps more enduring ‚Äî strategic non-alignment.

This isn‚Äôt the return of Cold War rivalry. It‚Äôs the rise of what policymakers are calling multi-alignment ‚Äî or more pointedly,¬Ýactive non-alignment. From Bras√≠lia to Jakarta, Ankara to Nairobi, governments are no longer organizing their foreign policy around loyalty. They‚Äôre organizing it around leverage.¬Ý

Rather than form bloc-based alternatives or pledge allegiance to rival powers, these states are playing the field ‚Äî engaging Washington where useful, Beijing where strategic, and building new horizontal alliances among themselves. It‚Äôs not anti-Americanism. It‚Äôs strategic flexibility.

The recent BRICS summit in Rio de Janeiro offered a quiet, elegant demonstration. While some expected fireworks or a bold anti-Western manifesto, the bloc instead issued the ‚ÄúRio de Janeiro Declaration,‚Äù a¬Ý31-page document¬Ýcalling for reform of UN and Bretton Woods institutions, ethical AI governance, and increased climate finance, while condemning attacks on Iran and supporting a Gaza ceasefire. The declaration noticeably steered clear of anti-U.S. rhetoric.

Lula, who hosted the summit, clarified the bloc‚Äôs ethos: BRICS, he argued, is not a tool of confrontation but¬Ýa platform for reform, drawing inspiration from the 1955 Bandung Conference. Lula emphasized ‚Äúequidistance‚Äù between major powers and reaffirmed Brazil‚Äôs non-aligned posture.

Brazil‚Äôs behavior reflected this balancing act. While hosting China, Russia, and expanding BRICS membership to include Egypt, Ethiopia, Indonesia, Iran and the UAE (with Saudi Arabia as a partner), it resisted¬ÝTrump‚Äôs tariff demands¬Ýwithout escalating retaliation. India‚Äôs posture echoes this ambivalence. Prime Minister Narendra Modi‚Äôs government continues to deepen defense cooperation with Washington but also embraced chairmanship of the 2026 BRICS summit, advocating a ‚Äúhumanity first‚Äù approach, and bolstered trade with China and the UAE, including a pledge to triple bilateral trade with Brazil. New Delhi now defines its foreign policy as ‚Äúissue-based engagement‚Äù ‚Äî hedging pragmatically without ideological commitment.

Turkey¬Ýoffers yet another illustration. While remaining a NATO member and participating in U.S. military procurement, Ankara sent Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan to Rio, affirming Turkey‚Äôs interest in deeper BRICS collaboration. Turkish officials frame their diplomacy as ‚Äúmulti-directional,‚Äù building influence from Brussels to Baku without fixed allegiance.

This is no longer anecdotal. According to¬Ýthe Munich Security Report 2025, 57 percent of surveyed Global South policymakers now describe their diplomacy as ‚Äúmulti-aligned‚Äù ‚Äî ¬Ýa 21-point increase since 2020. These governments are not rejecting the U.S. or embracing its rivals. They‚Äôre diversifying, recalibrating and insulating their interests.

Why now? The answer lies in the lived reality of Trump‚Äôs second term. His foreign policy decisions have triggered uncertainty, friction and strategic de-risking ‚Äî even among long-standing allies.

First, Trump withdrew the United States from¬Ýthe Paris climate agreement¬Ýon his first day in office. Citing unfair burdens on American industry, the move sent shockwaves through global climate diplomacy ‚Äî halting billions in climate finance and stalling joint decarbonization frameworks. In response, BRICS called for $300 billion annually by 2035 from developed nations to support Global South climate goals.

Second, his administration reinstated¬ÝSection 232 tariffs¬Ýon steel and aluminum imports from 12 countries, including Brazil, South Africa, India and the European Union. By April, tariff rates ranged between 25 percent and 35 percent, prompting diplomatic protests and early signs of trade retaliation.

Third,¬ÝTrump halted¬ÝU.S. contributions to the World Bank‚Äôs clean energy lending platform, suspending America‚Äôs role in shaping development finance norms and accelerating alternatives among Global South states.

Fourth, he¬Ýfroze diplomatic aid¬Ýto non-NATO countries, labeling them ‚Äúsecurity freeloaders‚Äù under his transactional doctrine. Governments in Africa, Southeast Asia and Latin America responded by pivoting toward regional solutions and partnerships.

Fifth ‚Äî and perhaps most destabilizing ‚Äî has been Trump‚Äôs tariff strategy itself. On July 6, he announced a 10 percent tariff¬Ýon all BRICS imports, effective Aug. 1, with threats of escalation to 25 percent to 40 percent if bilateral deals fail, and 100 percent tariffs if BRICS nations reduce dollar usage. A 50 percent tariff was specifically imposed on Brazil, citing its hosting of the summit and alleged ‚Äúattacks‚Äù on U.S. tech firms. The July 9 deadline for revised trade protocols,¬Ýbranded¬Ý‚ÄúTariff D-Day,‚Äù was extended to Aug. 1, prompting emergency negotiations and capital flight.

According to¬Ýrecent IMF estimates, foreign direct investment inflows to the U.S. declined 6.1 percent in the first quarter of 2025. Meanwhile, Vietnam, Japan and Mexico initiated regional currency talks and trade hedging mechanisms to reduce exposure to U.S. sanctions.

In response, Global South nations are increasingly turning to each other ‚Äî not out of hostility toward the U.S., but out of necessity. Kenya and India launched a joint climate credit platform.

Mexico and Colombia expanded investment agreements with China and the UAE, bypassing dollar-clearing systems. China and Vietnam agreed to advance railway cooperation, while China and the United Arab Emirates signaled investments in Brazil‚Äôs Tropical Forests Forever Facility. The BRICS Multilateral Guarantee mechanism, launched by the New Development Bank, aims to boost infrastructure and climate investments.

Even traditional Western partners are rethinking their footing. Germany‚Äôs foreign office, in an internal memo, warned that Washington‚Äôs offer of partnership is shrinking. France convened a strategic sovereignty dialogue with Brazil and Egypt. These aren‚Äôt alliances dissolving. They‚Äôre alliances mutating.

Testimony before the U.S. Senate Foreign Relations Committee this spring further validated these trends.¬ÝJoseph Ledford, senior fellow at the Hoover Institution, declared: ‚ÄúWashington‚Äôs reliance on loyalty has outpaced its offering of leadership.‚Äù

That‚Äôs the crux of this new era: strategic autonomy isn‚Äôt ideological. It‚Äôs adaptive. Governments are resisting dependency, not relationships. They‚Äôre refusing exclusivity ‚Äì not engagement.

None of this amounts to an anti-American bloc. The Global South doesn‚Äôt want confrontation. It wants co-authorship. The BRICS expansion ‚Äî now representing¬Ý45 percent of global population¬Ýand 35 percent of GDP ‚Äî is not a Cold War redux. It‚Äôs a forum for pluralism, not polarity.

And that presents an opportunity. For the U.S., the challenge is not competition. It‚Äôs relevance. If Washington can embrace multipolar diplomacy ‚Äî grounded in mutual respect and institutional reform ‚Äî it still holds the tools for leadership. But if it continues to demand allegiance while offering volatility, it risks being sidelined not by adversaries, but by indifference.

In Trump‚Äôs second term, America still matters. But increasingly, the world no longer waits. The U.S. can still lead, but only by listening. It must join the world‚Äôs new diplomatic table ‚Äî ¬Ýnot demand to own it.

Imran Khalid is a physician and has a master‚Äôs degree in international relations.

data.title: Bessent: Tariffs should boost US manufacturing ‚Äòover the next couple of years‚Äô
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said President Trump‚Äôs tariffs should boost American manufacturing over the ‚Äúnext couple of years.‚Äù¬Ý

‚ÄúWe‚Äôre seeing these very large commitments both from governments and from corporates,‚Äù Bessent said Thursday while on MSNBC‚Äôs ‚ÄúMorning Joe,‚Äù adding later that ‚Äúwe‚Äôve got trillions and trillions of manufacturing that‚Äôs going to come back, and we‚Äôre going to see that in the next couple of years.‚Äù

Trump‚Äôs reciprocal tariffs formally kicked in Thursday, impacting shipments of goods from both trading allies and adversaries.¬Ý

‚ÄúIT‚ÄôS MIDNIGHT!!! BILLIONS OF DOLLARS IN TARIFFS ARE NOW FLOWING INTO THE UNITED STATES OF AMERICA,‚Äù Trump wrote just before midnight on Truth Social.¬Ý

Some countries will face steep tariff rates, including Syria (41 percent), Laos (40 percent) and Switzerland (39 percent).¬ÝOthers like Thailand and Indonesia were dealt a 19 percent tariff, while South Korea, Japan and the European Union will grapple with a 15 percent rate.¬Ý

The president also said, ‚ÄúTHE ONLY THING THAT CAN STOP AMERICA‚ÄôS GREATNESS WOULD BE A RADICAL LEFT COURT THAT WANTS TO SEE OUR COUNTRY FAIL.‚Äù¬Ý

Bessent, one of Trump‚Äôs main trade negotiators, said inflation has been on a downturn.¬Ý

‚ÄúSo overall, inflation is down, and what we‚Äôre seeing is the manufacturers overseas are absorbing some of that, retailers are absorbing some of that, and part of our plan is to have real income growth for working class Americans,‚Äù he said Thursday morning.¬Ý

The Treasury chief said that during Trump‚Äôs first White House term, hourly workers did ‚Äúmuch better‚Äù than supervisory workers, and the same might occur again.¬Ý

‚ÄúWe saw the bottom 50 percent of households in terms of assets, their asset growth outperformed the top 10 percent so I would expect we‚Äôd see that again,‚Äù he said.¬Ý

data.title: An Ohio dealership repossessed her car, so she took their name and then sued
data.description:
LIMA, Ohio (WCMH) ‚Äî After a woman‚Äôs vehicle was repossessed by an Ohio dealership, she decided to take a unique form of retribution.

Tiah McCreary purchased a vehicle from Taylor Kia of Lima in February 2024. She signed the finance paperwork, which included an agreement to settle legal disputes through arbitration, according to court documents.

EV automaker sues Ohio over direct sales ban, wants to sell like Tesla

She applied for financing through Global Lending Services with the help of a finance manager and received a preliminary loan approval for the required amount.

McCreary left the dealership in a 2022 Kia K5, but GLS later determined that her income information was not sufficient to complete the loan, according to the documents. Because she no longer had the financing, the dealership repossessed the car in March.

The woman was exploring her legal options when she noticed that the name ‚ÄúTaylor Kia of Lima‚Äù was no longer registered with the Ohio Secretary of State‚Äôs Office because Taylor Cadillac did not submit a renewal application, according to documents. McCreary decided to register ‚ÄúTaylor Kia of Lima‚Äù under her name.

McCreary sent a cease and desist letter to the dealership, informing it that the name was registered to her.

She then filed a complaint against Taylor Cadillac ‚Äî which opened the Kia dealership in 2012 ‚Äî and the lending company, accusing them of violating the Consumer Sales Practices Act. Months later, Taylor Cadillac and GLS filed a motion for arbitration and included an arbitration agreement signed by McCreary.

Report rates Ohio Supreme Court as ‚Äòpoor‚Äô when it comes to gift disclosures

In October 2024, a trial court granted Taylor Cadillac‚Äôs motion for arbitration and dismissed the case without prejudice, court documents show.

McCreary appealed later that month and argued that her signature was placed on the arbitration agreement without her knowledge and that it was invalid because the ‚ÄúTaylor Kia of Lima‚Äù name was not registered. She also argued that the arbitration agreement for purchasing a vehicle should not apply to the use of ‚ÄúTaylor Kia of Lima,‚Äù which was now registered to McCreary.

Judge John R. Willamowski ruled that McCreary should have known she was signing an arbitration agreement and noted that the arbitration agreement stated it was between the purchaser and ‚ÄúTaylor Automotive Group, which includes Taylor Cadillac‚Ä¶,‚Äù therefore, the agreement was valid.

However, the judge did rule that the dispute over the use of the name ‚ÄúTaylor Kia of Lima‚Äù does not fall within the scope of the arbitration agreement because it is not related to the purchase of the vehicle.

Columbus zoo, township battle over safety tax

‚Äú[T]his claim should not have been dismissed and sent to arbitration,‚Äù Willamowski wrote.

The judge remanded the case to the trial court for further proceedings. Judge Juergen A. Waldick and Judge William R. Zimmerman concurred with Willamowski‚Äôs opinion.

A scheduling conference is scheduled for Aug. 18 to determine the next steps in the case.

data.title: Trump to sign executive order allowing cryptocurrencies, private equity in 401(k)s
data.description:
President Trump is set to sign an executive order on Thursday to allow 401(k) investors to access cryptocurrencies, private equity, real estate and other digital and alternative assets.

The order directs the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to revise regulations in order to facilitate access to alternative assets for participant-directed defined-contribution retirement savings plans, including 401(k)s.

Trump, through the order, would direct Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer to reexamine the guidance on fiduciary‚Äôs duties regarding alternative asset investments and direct her to consult with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and the SEC to determine what regulatory changes can be made.

Trump is signing the order to give American workers more investment options, a White House official said, arguing that alternative assets like private equity, real estate, and digital assets offer competitive returns and diversification benefits.

An order that would encourage retirement funds to invest in private equity comes as that sector is hungry for capital after shrinking last year for the first time in decades. Through the order, Trump would be opening up retirement plans to riskier investments while giving private equity a much-needed capital infusion.

The customers of private equity firms are usually big institutional investors like defined-benefit pension plans, along with super wealthy people, while defined-contribution retirement plans usually invest in typically safer publicly traded stocks and bonds.

The president has taken other steps to diversify financial options for Americans, including rolling back Biden-era rules on digital assets and dismissing cases and investigation against numerous crypto cases.¬Ý

Trump promised on the campaign trail to make the U.S. the ‚Äúcrypto capital of the planet.‚Äù The White House last month laid out in a 166-page report with recommendations for lawmakers and regulators on everything from crypto oversight to taxation to banking rules.¬Ý¬Ý

Tobias Burns and Julia Shapero contributed to this report.

data.title: Eli Lilly weight loss pill shows promise, but not as strong as injectables
data.description:
Eli Lilly‚Äôs experimental oral weight loss pill helped patients lose about 12 percent of their body weight on average in a late-stage trial, the company said Thursday, positive results but still less than its injectable competitors.¬Ý

Lilly‚Äôs stock was down more than 14 percent on the day after the results were announced.¬Ý

After 72 weeks, patients taking the highest dose of the drug, called orforglipron, lost an average of 12.4 percent of their weight, or 27.3 pounds, in a 72-week trial. ¬Ý

When analyzing all participants regardless of discontinuations, patients experienced weight loss of 11.2 percent.¬Ý

While promising, the results are not as strong as the two injectable drugs already on the market.

Orforglipron has not been directly compared with Wegovy or Zepbound, but trials of Novo Nordisk‚Äôs Wegovy showed a 15 percent weight loss, and patients taking Lilly‚Äôs Zepbound experienced a 21 percent weight loss.¬Ý

‚ÄúWith orforglipron, we‚Äôre working to transform obesity care by introducing a potential once-daily oral therapy that could support early intervention and long-term disease management, while offering a convenient alternative to injectable treatments,‚Äù Kenneth Custer, executive vice president and president of Lilly Cardiometabolic Health said in a statement.¬Ý

The company is planning to submit orforglipron for Food and Drug Administration review by the end of the year. If approved, Lilly said it is confident in its ability to launch the drug worldwide without supply constraints. ¬Ý

Expectations for the drug were high after the company in April reported orforglipron helped cut blood sugar and weight in a late-stage trial of people with type 2 diabetes. ¬Ý

A needle-free anti-obesity drug without dietary restrictions could give Lilly a major edge over its well-established rivals, but the latest data could significantly temper that.¬Ý

GLP-1s are a class of drugs that have become blockbusters for the way they help people lose weight. But they are expensive, must be refrigerated and are delivered through an injection. A daily pill version could make the drugs much more accessible.¬Ý

Lilly‚Äôs pill is also a small molecule drug, so it can be more easily manufactured.¬Ý

Side effects were the same as those with the injectable obesity drugs ‚Äî diarrhea, indigestion, constipation, nausea and vomiting. But the trial saw a nearly 25 percent dropout rate among people taking the drug at its highest dose.¬Ý

Still, CEO David Ricks told CNBC‚Äôs Squawk Box that the trial results were not disappointing.¬Ý

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs right on thesis for us,‚Äù Ricks said. ‚ÄúThe goal was to create an oral pill that was convenient and can be made at a huge scale, really, for the mass market, and had weight loss that was competitive with other single-acting GLP-1s, and that‚Äôs what we‚Äôve achieved.‚Äù¬Ý

data.title: Trump administration fires ex-acting FBI director seen as champion of rank-and-file staff
data.description:
The FBI is firing the former acting director who led the bureau at the start of the Trump administration.

Brian Driscoll, who has since returned to a career post at the bureau, has been asked to leave by Friday, a source familiar confirmed to The Hill.¬Ý

Driscoll has been viewed as a champion of the bureau‚Äôs rank-and-file staff. He declined to turn over a list of the thousands of FBI agents who worked on investigations into those that stormed the Capitol on Jan. 6.¬Ý

The request to do so sparked a lawsuit from FBI staff, who feared that the list would be used to target agents and could lead to retribution.

The FBI declined to comment.

Driscoll‚Äôs removal appears to be part of a wider purge.

Other outlets reported Thursday that Steve Jensen, the acting director in charge of the Washington Field Office,¬Ýwas asked to leave¬Ýas well as Walter Giardina, an agent who worked on a number of cases involving President Trump. Giardina worked for special counsel Robert Mueller and aided in the prosecution of Peter Navarro, a White House aide at the time who is now an adviser to Trump on trade.

Giardina has been a target of Senate Judiciary Chair Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa), who asked the FBI and the Justice Department to¬Ýturn over a number of documents¬Ýabout his work.

The move is likely to hurt morale at the FBI, which has seen the firing of a number of career leaders.

The Trump administration in February¬Ýforced out¬Ýthe five highest career positions at the FBI along with the heads of numerous field offices, including the then-leader in Washington, D.C.

Driscoll pushed back when asked to turn over the list of agents to Emil Bove, then serving in the No. 3 role at the Justice Department.

‚ÄúWe understand that this request encompasses thousands of employees across the country who have supported these investigative efforts,‚Äù Driscoll wrote in an email to staff at the time.

‚ÄúI am one of those employees.‚Äù

Driscoll has been with the FBI for over 20 years, recently serving as the head of the Newark Field Office. He also previously served on the FBI‚Äôs elite Hostage Rescue Team.

Known internally as Drizz, his pushback on the Trump administration sparked¬Ýnumerous memes in his honor, with one depicting him as a saint and another asking ‚ÄúWhat Would Drizz Do?‚Äù

data.title: ‚ÄòSouth Park‚Äô mocks Noem, Vance in new episode
data.description:
‚ÄúSouth Park‚Äù on Wednesday night continued its satirical attacks on the Trump administration, with its latest episode taking aim at figures including the president, Vice President Vance and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem.¬Ý

The episode, dubbed ‚ÄúGot a Nut,‚Äù depicts the school counselor, Mr. Mackey, who is terminated from his job at an elementary school, eventually joining Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE).¬Ý

Mackey watches a recruitment video where Noem refers to shooting her dog, then shows her shooting multiple dogs. Later in the episode, Noem is shown leading an immigration raid on a ‚ÄúDora the Explorer live concert, where she shoots a dog, and then a raid in heaven, where she tells agents to ‚Äúonly detain the brown ones.‚Äù

Mackey is later invited to Trump‚Äôs Mar-a-Lago resort, where the president offers to make him Noem‚Äôs replacement, then invites the former school counselor into his bed with satan.¬Ý

Vance is depicted as a mini-sized Trump servant. After Trump gets annoyed with Vance in the episode, the president kicks him.¬Ý

‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôve finally made it,‚Äù Vance said in a Thursday morning post on social media platform X, commenting on an image of his portrayal in the episode.

‚ÄúSouth Park‚Äù and the administration have gone back-and-forth recently, with the sitcom jabbing DHS for using an image from the cartoon to help their ICE recruiting efforts.

‚ÄúWait, so we ARE relevant,‚Äù the show wrote on Tuesday on X, before telling DHS to ‚Äúeat a bag of d‚Äë‚Äë‚Äës.‚Äù

‚ÄúWe want to thank South Park for drawing attention to ICE law enforcement recruitment: We are calling on patriotic Americans to help us remove murderers, gang members, pedophiles, and other violent criminals from our country,‚Äù DHS said in a statement earlier this week.

data.title: Religious freedom is at a crossroads in US foreign policy¬Ý
data.description:
It is both ironic and tragic that the Department of State ‚Äãhas ‚Äã‚Äãimplemented‚Äã‚Äã a massive reorganization, ‚Äãwhich may have troubling implications for ‚ÄãAmerica‚Äôs international religious freedom policy.

As recently approved, the reorganization subordinates the International Religious Freedom Office to the agenda and operational authority of the human rights bureau, foreshadowing a return of religious liberty to its former bureaucratic isolation.‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã ¬Ý ¬Ý ‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã ‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã¬Ý

If this move stands, it ‚Äãmay‚Äã harm international religious freedom policy during the Trump administration, and provide a dangerous precedent that the next progressive secretary of State will certainly exploit.

Here‚Äôs the irony: In his first term, Trump issued an¬Ýexecutive order¬Ýaccurately declaring that religious freedom is a ‚Äúmoral and national security imperative,‚Äù and ordering steps to strengthen America‚Äôs largely ineffective international religious freedom policy.¬Ý

That policy, which was established in 1998 by the¬ÝInternational Religious Freedom Act, had languished within the State Department‚Äôs liberal bureaucracy since its passage.¬Ý

It began to flourish under Trump‚Äôs international religious freedom ambassador, Sam Brownback, when the International Religious Freedom Office was elevated out of the bureaucracy and finally given a degree of authority and responsibility commensurate with its foreign policy significance.¬Ý¬Ý

The tragedy is that the vast potential of this policy, both for the global victims of cruel religious persecution and for the national security of the United States, may again go unfulfilled ‚Äãwith ‚Äãthe International Religious Freedom Office and the ambassador ‚Äãreturned to ‚Äã‚Äãtheir former station within the State Department‚Äôs vast bureaucracy‚Äã.¬Ý

To understand why this is so, a bit of history is in order. The International Religious Freedom Act created the position of ambassador at large for international religious freedom, with the mission of ‚Äúadvancing religious freedom‚Äù as America‚Äôs founders understood it ‚Äî an inalienable right that warranted protection for all peaceful religious individuals and communities.¬Ý

Democrats have long rejected this understanding. In 1998, Secretary of State Madeleine Albright¬Ýmade the claim¬Ýthat international religious freedom creates an illegitimate ‚Äúhierarchy of human rights,‚Äù enabling religious freedom to endanger other human rights.¬Ý¬Ý

Albright placed the international religious freedom ambassador and the office under the human rights bureau; State‚Äôs repository of progressive rights, such as abortion and gay marriage. It remained there for almost two decades.¬Ý¬Ý

In 2016, Sen. Marco Rubio (R-Fla.) cosponsored amendments to the International Religious Freedom Act to fix the problem by increasing the authority of the ambassador at large.¬Ý

No longer embedded in the human rights bureau, the ambassador was now ‚Äúto report directly to the secretary of State,‚Äù with the authority ‚Äúto coordinate international religious freedom policies‚Äù throughout the government. These changes coincided with Trump‚Äôs first election and set the stage for Ambassador Brownback.¬Ý¬Ý

Brownback used his authority to demonstrate the practical ‚Äúmoral and national security‚Äù role that U.S. international religious freedom policy can play.¬Ý

He enticed scores of foreign ministers and thousands of civil society leaders to Washington to learn why the U.S. was committed to international religious freedom and how it could benefit their own nations.

They heard the case that religious freedom is an inalienable right¬Ýand at the core of human dignity, that it combats terrorism, that it can limit the powers of government and discourage external aggression, that nations committed to respecting religious freedom prosper significantly more than nations that do not.¬Ý¬Ý

Brownback traveled the world to make these arguments. He roundly condemned the worst persecutors, including China‚Äôs Xi Jinping, Iran‚Äôs theocratic Ayatollahs, ISIS and Taliban terrorists and Russia‚Äôs Vladimir Putin.

He demonstrated that a bold and robust U.S. international religious freedom policy can benefit human rights and human dignity for everyone, and can benefit America‚Äôs national security.¬Ý

Under the last administration, however, Secretary of State Antony Blinken¬Ýdeclared¬Ýthat human rights were ‚Äúco-equal,‚Äù that is, that there are no inalienable rights. The enhanced authority of Biden‚Äôs international religious freedom ambassador, given him by the 2016 International Religious Freedom Act amendments, was undercut.¬Ý

But Republican concessions to Democrats in the amendments, namely placing the rights of ‚Äúatheists and humanists‚Äù in the International Religious Freedom Act, were vigorously pursued by Blinken‚Äôs human rights bureau.¬ÝPrograms promoting atheism were funded under the auspices of ‚Äúreligious freedom.‚Äù¬Ý

‚Äã‚ÄãEarlier this year, Trump named religious freedom champion Marco Rubio as his secretary of State, and the future of international religious freedom seemed bright again.‚Äã¬Ý

And, the ‚Äã‚Äãfinal‚Äã‚Äã rollout of the reorganization has revealed the designation of an undersecretary for Foreign Assistance, Humanitarian Affairs and Religious Freedom, a leadership position which had not previously existed in that form.‚Äã¬Ý

But ‚Äãthese salutary developments‚Äã ‚Äãdo not change the fact that ‚Äãthe international religious freedom ambassador and his office ‚Äã‚Äã‚Äãshould not have ‚Äãbeen‚Äã moved back under the human rights bureau. ‚ÄãIt is unclear how the reorganization is compatible with the 2016 International Religious Freedom Act amendments. ‚Äã‚Äã

What, then, can be done?¬Ý¬Ý

First, ‚ÄãState Department leadership ‚Äã‚Äãmust preserve ‚Äãthe full authority of the international religious freedom ambassador given him by law‚Äã amid these sweeping structural changes.‚Äã ‚ÄãThe ‚Äãvalue‚Äã of the ambassador‚Äôs work‚Äã to America and the world‚Äã was‚Äã clearly demonstrated by Brownback.¬Ý¬Ý

Second,‚Äã such a massive reorganization should be followed by a ‚Äã‚Äãmandatory, ‚Äã‚Äãcomprehensive assessment ‚Äã‚Äãat six months ‚Äã‚Äãof its efficacy across State‚Äôs major functions and policy areas. ‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã‚ÄãWhen that time comes, the liabilities of placing the‚Äã International Religious Freedom Office ‚Äãback in the human ‚Äã‚Äãrights ‚Äã‚Äãbureau ‚Äã‚Äãshould be scrutinized by House and Senate committees with jurisdiction.¬Ý

If the operational authority of the International Religious Freedom Office has been compromised and its ability to shape foreign policy limited by ‚Äãbureaucracy‚Äã, then the office should be returned to its place directly under the secretary of State.‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã ¬Ý ¬Ý ‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã‚Äã ‚Äã‚Äã¬Ý¬Ý

‚Äã‚ÄãFinally, ‚Äãhistory suggests that the precedent this reorganization is establishing may pave the way for a‚Äã future administration to ‚Äãsideline‚Äã U.S. international religious freedom policy yet again.

State Department leadership must proceed with extreme caution in this reorganization to avoid the mistakes of their predecessors, who all-too-often weakened U.S. international religious freedom policy by isolating it within the State Department‚Äôs enormous bureaucracy. ‚Äã¬Ý

Thomas Farr was the first director of the Office of International Religious Freedom, serving under Ambassadors Robert Seiple (Clinton) and John Hanford (Bush). He is president emeritus of the Religious Freedom Institute. David Trimble is president of the Religious Freedom Institute.¬Ý

data.title: Suspect in Minnesota lawmaker shootings pleads not guilty to federal charges
data.description:
The man accused of¬Ýshooting two Minnesota state lawmakers¬Ýand their spouses, killing one couple, on Thursday pleaded not guilty in federal court.

Vance Boelter faces six counts including murder for allegedly assassinating former Minnesota Democratic House Speaker Melissa Hortman and her husband, Mark, in June. He‚Äôs also accused of shooting state Sen. John Hoffman (D) and his wife, Yvette, who survived their injuries.

The charges could carry the death penalty.

At a 10-minute arraignment Thursday, Boelter‚Äôs federal defender, Manvir Atwal, entered a not guilty plea for the 58-year-old Minnesota native, according to NewsNation, The Hill‚Äôs sister network.

Boelter appeared in court unshackled, wearing a yellow jumpsuit and orange sweatshirt and speaking only to address the court.

U.S. Magistrate Judge Dulce Foster approved prosecutors‚Äô motion to designate the proceedings a ‚Äúcomplex case‚Äù in order to waive standard speedy trial requirements, so that each side can review the ample evidence ‚Äî tens of thousands of pages of discovery and hundreds of gigabytes of media files, the government said.

In addition to the murder counts, Boelter faces stalking charges over his alleged efforts to track down both¬Ýstate lawmakers and firearms violations for each shooting. The indictment, filed last month, indicates that Boelter attempted to shoot Hoffman‚Äôs daughter, Hope, as well.

Authorities found a notebook with the names of more than 45 elected officials in a search of Boelter‚Äôs car. He was taken into custody after a two-day manhunt involving nearly 200 law enforcement officers, the state‚Äôs largest manhunt ever.

A trial date has not yet been set, and prosecutors said a decision on the death penalty is months away.

data.title: Map: Who may see northern lights as coronal mass ejection hits Earth
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) ‚Äî Several states, primarily along the northern border, may have the chance to see the northern lights on Thursday.

A coronal mass ejection, or CME, was hurled out by the sun on Tuesday, NOAA‚Äôs Space Weather Prediction Center said. The CME, paired with a coronal hole high speed stream, or CH HSS, is ‚Äúexpected to cause geomagnetic storming‚Äù Thursday into Friday, according to an alert from the Space Weather Prediction Center.

CMEs are explosions of plasma and magnetic material from the sun, while a CH HSS refers to escaping solar winds from areas on the sun that are cooler and less dense. Both are capable of sparking auroras that we can see on Earth.

Mall-based retailer Claire‚Äôs files for bankruptcy again

Current forecasting shows the strongest geomagnetic storming could happen Thursday, prompting a G2-level watch. Conditions are expected to weaken Friday, giving way to a G1-level watch.

At these lower levels ‚Äî geomagnetic storms are classified based on their strength along a five-point scale ‚Äî the associated auroral activity may only be visible to those in the upper regions of the U.S.

The map below shows where the northern lights may be visible on Thursday. With the exception of Alaska, the likelihood of seeing the aurora in the Lower 48 is slim and limited to parts or all of Illinois, Iowa, Idaho, Maine, Michigan, Minnesota, Montana, Nebraska, New Hampshire, New York, North Dakota, South Dakota, Vermont, Washington, Wisconsin, and Wyoming.

Those along or near the red view line on the map may only catch a glimpse of the northern lights on the northern horizon.

What still causes the plague in the US?

The forecasting is relatively similar for Friday. Parts or all of several states may again have the chance to see the northern lights: Iowa, Idaho, Maine, Michigan, Minnesota, Montana, New Hampshire, New York, North Dakota, South Dakota, Vermont, Washington, Wisconsin, and Wyoming.

The strength of the geomagnetic activity and the timing of it could impact the aurora forecast.

According to the SWPC, conditions are expected to strengthen between 7 p.m. ET Thursday and about 1 a.m. ET Friday. They may weaken through the day on Friday before ramping up again around 7 p.m. ET.

When the northern lights forecast is weaker, it‚Äôs often recommended to get as far away from outdoor lights as possible and look northward. Even then, you may only be able to see the northern lights by taking a picture with your phone.
